text: Today I am presenting a case of laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a female, fifty years, with acute calculus cholecystitis.
The surgeon inserts a 10mm port at the umbilicus, which serves as the camera port for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon places a second 10mm port at the epigastrium, a third 5mm port at the midclavicular line, and a fourth 5mm port at the anterior axillary line, establishing access points for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder using a 5mm port at the anterior axillary line, holding it at the Hartmann's pouch. Dissection begins at the cystic duct, removing fibro-fatty tissue and visceral peritoneum to skeletonize the duct.
The surgeon focuses dissection on the posterior part of Calot's triangle, where no vascular or vital structures are present, ensuring a safer approach in this area.

The surgeon completes dissection of the posterior part of Calot's triangle and proceeds to dissect tissue over the anterior part, creating a window from anterior to posterior through Calot's triangle.
The surgeon attempts to create a window through the anterior part of the Calot's triangle, continuing the dissection from anterior to posterior.

After creating a window in the Callot's triangle, the surgeon identifies the bleeding cystic artery. Using a bipolar Maryland forceps, the surgeon cauterizes and cuts the artery, utilizing the forceps' ability to simultaneously coagulate and cut the tissue.
Following the coagulation and cutting of the cystic artery, the surgeon continues dissection towards the gallbladder fossa, removing fibro-fatty tissue within the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety in laparoscopic cholecystectomy by ensuring the liver bed is visible above and below the cystic duct, confirming the cystic duct's orientation from medial to lateral.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to place titanium clips on the cystic duct, applying two clips on the patient side and one clip on the specimen side. After securing the clips, the cystic duct is cut between the clips.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, taking slow and cautious steps due to its close proximity to the liver bed caused by acute calculus cholecystitis. The dissection is performed to avoid penetrating the liver tissue, which could result in significant bleeding.
The surgeon completes the dissection and proceeds to remove the entire gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, carefully separating it from the liver bed.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the surgical field, ensuring control of bleeding during the dissection of the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder specimen through the 10mm port at the epigastrium using a gallbladder extractor.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, carefully separating it from the liver bed due to acute calculus cholecystitis.
The surgeon irrigates the operated area and surrounding tissues with normal saline, inspecting for any signs of bleeding or bile leakage.
